# Passage:  
The nucleophilic alkyl substitution reactions are among the most thoroughly studied transformations in organic chemistry. Asubstitution by an SN2 reaction mechanismis shown in Figure 1 and asubstitution by an SN1mechanism is shown in Figure 2.  
![CP-9-1-115_01060-00P1.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-115_01060-00P1.gif)  
|**Figure 1**|SN2 Reaction mechanism|  
![CP-9-1-115_01060-00P2.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-115_01060-00P2.gif)  
|**Figure 2**|SN1 Reaction mechanism|  
The SN2 mechanism involves adirect displacement of a leaving group(L)by a nucleophile(N). The reaction results in an inversion of configurationat the carbon bearing the leaving group, because the incoming nucleophile attacks it from the side opposite the leaving group. The SN1 reaction mechanism involves anintermediate carbocationthat is formed by loss of the leaving group. Anucleophile can react with the planar carbocation from either side, leading to the inversion or retention of the configuration at the site of the substitution.  
The product distribution of two reactions is shown in figures 3 and 4.  
![CP-9-1-115_01060-00P3.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-115_01060-00P3.gif)  
|**Figure 3**|SN2 Reaction of 1-pentanol|  
![CP-9-1-115_01060-00P4.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-115_01060-00P4.gif)  
|**Figure 4**|Reaction of 2-methyl-2-butanol|  
Therate of the reaction shownin Figure 3increases as the concentration ofalcohol, halide, or H+increases. Therate of the reactionshown in Figure 4increases as the concentration ofalcohol or H+increases.This reaction is independent of the halide concentration.
